title: Try one thing that made you happy as a teenager
subtitle: A one-week activity for reconnecting with pleasure before it needed to be useful
excerpt: This week's activity: identify one thing you loved doing as a teenager, before enjoyment had to justify itself, and do a version of it. Not the same intensity, not a project. Just the thing itself.

practice.whenToUse: When the sense of what you actually enjoy has become unclear under the weight of adult responsibilities and productivity norms

content.text: A one-week activity. The premise is simple: somewhere before adulthood, most people had things they loved to do that didn't need to be useful. Those things are often still there as preferences, buried under years of productivity norms and limited time.

Think back to your teenage years. Not necessarily a happy time, but when enjoyment existed without having to justify itself. What did you do then that you don't do now? A specific art form, a sport, a type of music, a creative practice, a way of spending time. Name it.

Not the full version. Not taking up the instrument seriously again, not joining a team, not making it a project. The smallest available version: listen to the music you played then, buy one set of cheap pencils and draw for twenty minutes, watch a game on your own, reread the book.

Schedule it like any other commitment. Thirty minutes somewhere in the week. Do it without turning it into an improvement project. Notice what it feels like to do something that serves no purpose other than that you once loved it.
